message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objectives</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Ali-M3, an artificial intelligence, analyses chest computed tomography (CT) and detects the likelihood of coronavirus disease (COVID-19) in the range of 0 to 1. It demonstrates excellent performance for the detection of COVID-19 patients with a sensitivity and specificity of 98.5 and 99.2%, respectively. However, Ali-M3 has not been externally validated. Our purpose is to evaluate the external validity of Ali-M3 using Japanese sequential sampling data.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>In this retrospective cohort study, COVID-19 infection probabilities were calculated using Ali-M3 in 617 symptomatic patients who underwent reverse transcription-polymerase chain reaction (RT-PCR) tests and chest CT for COVID-19 diagnosis at 11 Japanese tertiary care facilities, between January 1 and April 15, 2020.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Of 617 patients, 289 patients (46.8%) were RT-PCR-positive. The area under the curve (AUC) of Ali-M3 for predicting a COVID-19 diagnosis was 0.797 (95% confidence intervals [CI]: 0.762-0.833) and goodness-of-fit was P = 0.156. With a cut-off of probability of COVID-19 by Ali-M3 diagnosis set at 0.5, the sensitivity and specificity were 80.6% and 68.3%, respectively, while a cut-off of 0.2 yielded a sensitivity and specificity of 89.2% and 43.2%, respectively. Among 223 patients who required oxygen support, the AUC was 0.825 and sensitivity at a cut-off of 0.5 and 0.2 were 88.7% and 97.9%, respectively. Although the sensitivity was lower when the days from symptom onset were few, sensitivity increased for both cut-off values after 5 days.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Ali-M3 was evaluated by external validation and shown to be useful to exclude a diagnosis of COVID-19.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Key Points</jats:title>
                  <jats:list list-type="order">
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>The area under the curve (AUC) of Ali-M3, which is an AI system for diagnosis of COVID-19 based on chest CT images, was 0.797 and goodness-of-fit was P = 0.156.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>With a cut-off of probability of COVID-19 by Ali-M3 diagnosis set at 0.5, the sensitivity and specificity were 80.6% and 68.3%, respectively, while a cut-off of 0.2 yielded 89.2% and 43.2%.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Although low sensitivity was observed in less number of days from symptoms onset, after 5 days high increasing sensitivity was observed. In patients requiring oxygen support, the AUC was higher that is 0.825.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                  </jats:list>
                </jats:sec>
title: Accuracy of deep learning based computed tomography diagnostic system of COVID-19: a consecutive sampling external validation cohort study
